title: rootless: detect no system session with --cgroup-manager=systemd

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *giuseppe* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [giuseppe] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
this should the issue @edsantiago had when polkit is not installed

The new behavior is:
```
# su - fedora
$ podman info
ERRO[0000] The cgroups manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available
ERRO[0000] You may need to enable lingering mode with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` as root
Error: systemd session not available
```
I think this is going to hurt a lot of people. Could I encourage you to reconsider this approach?

What I was trying to explain this morning is:
* rootless podman _works just fine_ without dbus systemd and all that stuff. Seriously, it really does.
* The "problem"—and I use quotes deliberately, because I think problem/feature depends on perspective—is when a user detaches and leaves behind a running container. There are workflows/environments in which this is desired, and some in which it is not.

I really think we need to leave that decision up to administrators. I do not think we (podman) should arbitrarily enforce one specific way.

Thinking out loud here (I haven't fully developed this idea): what if this test were made nonfatal, and moved only into `podman run -d` and `podman start` logic? Is there any other instance in which we care about persistence? (Hmmm. One immediate problem I see is that default log level is error, not warn, so we can't use logrus. That's not necessarily a showstopper though.)

I know this makes more work for everyone. I'm sorry, but I hope we all understand that this needs to be carefully thought out early.

> What I was trying to explain this morning is:
> 
>     * rootless podman _works just fine_ without dbus systemd and all that stuff. Seriously, it really does.
> 
>     * The "problem"—and I use quotes deliberately, because I think problem/feature depends on perspective—is when a user detaches and leaves behind a running container. There are workflows/environments in which this is desired, and some in which it is not.

We need to make sure `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs` still works as before.

Rootless containers, moving to cgroups v2, default to using `--cgroup-manager=systemd` so that cgroups can be used and managed by the unprivileged user.  We can make the error message clearer and also state `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs` as a solution but I don't think we can just ignore the issue
I am fine with this, but I want @edsantiago to approve.

As an end user, I still don't like this. It arbitrarily and capriciously breaks something that has worked for a very long time. 

If I were a new podman user, I would find the error annoying: why is podman complaining about this, why can't I just get it to work? (Or, why can't podman just set itself up to use the right cgroup-manager by itself?) As an existing podman user, I would be upset at having it suddenly start breaking on workflows that were fine for many months. 

AIUI this cgroups thing only matters if a rootless user wants to run detached containers. Is that correct, or am I misunderstanding something?  If so why are we punishing everyone, including users who never run `podman run -d`?

> AIUI this cgroups thing only matters if a rootless user wants to run detached containers. Is that correct, or am I misunderstanding something? If so why are we punishing everyone, including users who never run `podman run -d`?

unfortunately it is not only that.  The real issue is that we cannot use systemd for managing cgroups, so we'd need to fallback to `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs`.  That means rootless cannot set limits for rootless containers.  I'd prefer we don't do this automatically, but inform the user about changing the configuration, but if there is no agreement on this part I can change it to fallback automatically.

> [limits]

Ah - thanks. I had missed that.

However, may I suggest that you're thinking about this from the perspective of a guru? You know all about cgroups, and know how to edit `libpod.conf`. But try thinking about it from the perspective of a simple user; one who doesn't know or care about cgroups, or runc/crun, or any podman internals. Someone who just wants to get started with podman, or at least continue an existing workflow without breaking. Such a someone might not want to type `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs` to all podman invocations, or set up an alias, or understand why it's needed sometimes but not others. I think we have an opportunity here to be kind to users, and we should at least consider it.

we can either fail with an error as this PR does, or fallback to `--cgroup-manager=cgroupfs`.  I am not sure either what is the best choice here, fallbacking to cgroupfs may look more user friendly, but then users will need to figure out what went wrong when they are not able to set limits, and the container processes are running under root-owned cgroups.

@rhatdan what do you think?
Lets discuss this on Thursday, at standup.
@rhatdan @edsantiago changed the patch to fallback to cgroupfs

> What's the actual problem with configuring resources with unified cgroupfs?

the only issue on the host is that the cgroupfs must be configured beforehand by root and the subtree must be correctly owned by the rootless user.

It should be fine inside of a container though, we should probably relax the error and let the OCI runtime fail if cgroupfs cannot be used instead of failing early here

Something broke this weekend, between 1e750f7 and ca4c24c.  Gating tests are now failing again, because all rootless podman commands are emitting the new warning:
```
$ podman images
WARN[0000] The cgroups manager is set to systemd but there is no systemd user session available
WARN[0000] For using systemd, you may need to login using an user session
WARN[0000] Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root)
WARN[0000] Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs
REPOSITORY   TAG   IMAGE ID   CREATED   SIZE
```
(I have, as root, already done that `loginctl` command). I wonder if something in #4352 reset or unset a magic XDG envariable?
